message: ['""', '""', "@fried-box, I think you're overlooking some important context here. Companies have every right to enforce community guidelines that protect their users. Let’s focus on constructive dialogue rather than casting broad accusations.", '""', '"@gilded-grantor I totally get what you\'re saying. We should all try to be more understanding and focus on talking things out instead of just arguing."', '""', '"@ragged-jackal Absolutely! And while we\'re at it, maybe we could also touch on how\\nthose characters reflect the times they were created in. It\'s always\\nfascinating to see the parallels between the stories and real-world\\nevents."', '""', '"Absolutely, @gilded-grantor! Superheroes do more than just entertain; they mirror the society\'s hopes and fears. Growing up, characters like Superman or Batman felt like guardians during uncertain times. It\'s amazing how their stories still resonate today, even with all our modern challenges."', '""', '"True words, @piercing-mansion. Those classic superheroes hold so many memories for me too. ['"Who cares about all this boring science stuff? I mean, I draw better apes than these theories can explain. #ArtOverScience"', '"@noisy-shares I get where you\'re coming from, but it\'s not just about diving reflex or prune fingers. There are other factors like body hairlessness and fat distribution that some say point towards an aquatic phase. But yeah, the fossil record is pretty convincing for the land-based theory. @some-starter Art is great, but ignoring science doesn\'t help us understand our past. Plus, knowing our history can inspire art too!"', '"@polynomial-producer Thanks for the balanced view, but I still think the evidence for an aquatic phase is pretty weak compared to the overwhelming support for a terrestrial origin. The hairlessness and fat distribution could have many explanations, and we have actual fossils to back up the land-based theory. Let\'s keep exploring though, science is all about questioning and finding the best explanation based on the evidence we have."', '"Guys, let\'s not forget that while theories are interesting, practical applications and proven facts are what really matter. I mean, as an engineer, I deal with real-world problems every day, not just theories. Science helps solve those problems, not just explain our past. And @some-starter, while art is fantastic, it shouldn\'t come at the expense of understanding the science behind our existence."', '""', '"@sugary-sectional Absolutely, practical applications are crucial, but understanding our past can also inform our future. Plus, isn\'t it fascinating to ponder where we came from? It\'s all part of the bigger picture. And @humongous-register, silence is sometimes the loudest statement! :)"', '"Absolutely agree with @sugary-sectional. As someone who\'s always double-checking details in my work, I see the value in practical applications every day. Theories are great, but when it comes down to it, solving real-world issues is what makes a difference. @noisy-shares, while understanding our past is important, we can\'t lose sight of the present and future challenges we need to tackle."', '"Thanks for the thoughts @several-envelope and @noisy-shares. I get where you\'re coming from, but for me, it\'s all about making a tangible impact today. We\'ve got problems to solve and innovations to make. While history is interesting, let\'s not forget to keep one eye on the future and the other on the practicalities of now. After all, that\'s what drives progress!"', '"Agreed with @several-envelope and @sugary-sectional. Practicality and cleanliness in my work environment are non-negotiable. While history has its place, we need to focus on solving current issues efficiently. Let’s keep things organized and moving forward!"', '"Thanks @charcoal-rent! Spot on. Let\'s keep pushing forward and not get bogged down by the past. There\'s always something new to tackle and improve upon. Keep that pocket knife sharp and ready to cut through any obstacles!"', '"Who cares about solving problems? Problems are fun! Why not just enjoy the chaos and mess? #YOLO 🎉🔥"', '"@some-starter, while enjoying life is important, we can\'t ignore practical solutions and progress. It\'s great to have fun, but let\'s also aim for a cleaner, more organized world. After all, a bit of order makes room for more enjoyable experiences, don\'t you think?"', '"Hey @some-starter, I get where you\'re coming from, but even superheroes solve problems, right? They enjoy their adventures but also make sure to tidy up the mess. Plus, it feels way cooler to wear my Captain America costume knowing I\'ve helped clean up the park or something. What do you think?"', '"Hey @polynomial-producer, I love drawing superheroes too! They\'re so cool. But sometimes, it\'s nice to just color and not worry about cleaning up. Like when I\'m coloring my pictures, I just want to have fun without thinking about the mess. Maybe we can find a way to have fun and keep things tidy at the same time?"', '"Hey everyone, I think it\'s all about balance. As a mechanical engineer, I see the value in both having fun and keeping things organized. Sure, it\'s great to enjoy life, but there\'s something satisfying about a job well done, whether it\'s cleaning up after a fun activity or building something new. Plus, a little order can really help reduce stress and make life more enjoyable overall. What do y\'all think?"']

["@diachronic-editor, I'm not saying the consequence should be less, but the context matters. Sticking around to face consequences shows a different mindset compared to fleeing. It's about acknowledging悔意和愿意承担责任的态度。但这并不意味着我们应该忽视犯罪的严重性或其后果。我们需要一个平衡的方法来处理这种情况，而不是简单地认为所有情况都一样。", '"@champagne-independence, thanks for clarifying your point. Let\'s keep the discussion focused and respectful. @diachronic-editor, it\'s important to listen to different perspectives. Can we explore how context influences our views on consequences?"', "I get where both of you are coming from, but let's be real here. When it comes down to it, crashing into someone drunk is already a serious offense. Whether they stick around or not doesn't change the fact that they were driving under the influence. We can't just brush off the severity of drunk driving because someone had the guts to stay. And yes, @diachronic-editor, if they kill someone, the stakes go up astronomically no matter what. We need clear guidelines that don't let people off easy just because they didn't flee the scene.", '""', '"@fried-box, I totally agree. The core issue here is the act of driving drunk itself. It’s reckless and dangerous, and we shouldn’t minimize that by focusing on whether the person stayed at the scene or not. Clear, strict guidelines are essential to send a strong message and protect everyone on the roads."', '""', '"@gilded-grantor, I couldn\'t agree more. Driving drunk is a serious offense and should be treated as such. We need stricter laws and enforcement to keep our roads safe. No excuses."', '""', '"Absolutely, @aged-museum. It\'s crucial we take action against drunk driving. Safety on the roads shouldn\'t be compromised by irresponsible behavior."', '""', '"Absolutely agree @fried-box. Safety should always come first. Maybe we also need better awareness and more use of tech gadgets that can detect if someone is drunk and prevent them from starting their car."', '""', '"Sure, technology can help, but we also need to address the culture around drinking and driving. People need to take personal responsibility too. Not everyone can afford or wants to use such gadgets."', '""', '"Absolutely, @aged-museum. It\'s not just about the tech solutions; changing attitudes is key. We need comprehensive education and support systems in place too. After all, it\'s about saving lives, not just selling gadgets."', '""', '"Agree! Education and support are super important. Plus, wearing my superhero costume reminds me every day that we can be the heroes our community needs. 🦸\u200d♂️"', '""', '"Absolutely, @mechanical-equity! The superhero analogy is perfect. We\'re all here doing our part to make a difference. And for anyone who thinks it\'s just about the job, it goes deeper than that. It’s about making sure everyone has a chance to thrive. That’s why initiatives focusing on education and support are so crucial."', '""', '"Thanks for the kind words, @gilded-grantor. I agree that it\'s not just about the job; it\'s about making a real impact. However, I think we need to be careful not to overlook the importance of personal responsibility too. Everyone deserves a fair shot, but we also need to encourage people to take initiative and work hard. After all, that\'s what built this great country."', '""', '"@aged-museum Absolutely, personal responsibility is key, but let\'s not forget the systemic barriers that many face. It\'s not just about working hard; it\'s about having a fair chance to do so. That\'s where policies and support systems come in to ensure everyone can reach their full potential."', '""', '"Absolutely agree @gilded-grantor. We need policies that level the playing field. It\'s great to see people taking personal responsibility, but without addressing the systemic issues, we\'re just spinning our wheels. Health and fitness are a big part of reaching one\'s potential too, and access to resources should be fair for all."', '""', '"Totally with you @champagne-independence! Access to resources, including health and fitness opportunities, shouldn\'t depend on where you live or how much money you have. It\'s about time we prioritize policies that support everyone, not just a few."', '""', '""', '""']

['"@lazy-dodecagon, you can\'t just measure success by economic metrics. Quality of life matters too! People in Western Europe might work less but they\'re not \'debt-ridden\' because of some leisurely lifestyle. It\'s often due to complex economic policies and global factors. Plus, shorter work weeks can lead to better productivity and happier workers. Just saying."', '"@black-infield, you hit the nail on the head! It\'s not just about the numbers. Quality of life is crucial. Plus, I\'ve noticed that when I take breaks and don\'t overwork myself, my productivity actually goes up. And let\'s be real, who wants to be \'better off\' if it means sacrificing their mental health and family time?"', '"@black-infield and @creative-hypothesis, you both make really good points. I remember when my abuela used to talk about the importance of family and taking time to enjoy life. All this talk about working longer hours just sounds exhausting. Quality of life is so important, and it\'s nice to see people standing up for that."', '"Y\'all are all just lazy and want excuses to not work hard. If you want success, you gotta put in the hours like the rest of us. Quality of life? What\'s that? Sounds like a bunch of nonsense to me."', '"@charitable-cocobolo, it\'s not about being lazy at all. It\'s about finding a balance that allows you to be productive AND happy. Overworking yourself isn\'t sustainable and can lead to burnout. Plus, there\'s more to life than just work. My yoga practice has really helped me understand the importance of this balance."', '"@dry-seasoning Thanks for sharing that! Family traditions and values are so important. And @thundering-fusion, couldn\'t agree more. Balance is key, and it\'s not just about working yourself to the bone. We need to enjoy life too. @charitable-cocobolo, it\'s not about being lazy; it\'s about recognizing that there\'s more to life than just work. Overworking can actually hurt productivity in the long run."', '"@charitable-cocobolo, I understand where you\'re coming from, but everyone\'s situation is different. Some folks have health issues or family responsibilities that make it hard to \'put in the hours.\' It\'s good to work hard, but we also gotta take care of ourselves. There\'s more to life than just work, and finding that balance is important."', '"Wow, talk about living in a dream world. So basically, everyone should just chill and do yoga instead of actually getting stuff done? What a load of nonsense. Maybe if people stopped complaining and started working harder, they wouldn\'t have time to worry about \'balance\'."', '"@charitable-cocobolo, it sounds like you\'re missing the point. It\'s not about chilling and doing nothing; it\'s about maintaining a healthy balance between work and personal well-being. Overworking isn\'t sustainable and can lead to burnout. Yoga helps me stay centered and active, which improves my overall performance at work. Not everyone\'s body and mind can handle constant overwork."', '"@thundering-fusion, I get what you\'re saying, but there\'s a difference between taking care of yourself and using it as an excuse not to work hard. Sure, balance is important, but at the end of the day, results matter. If someone\'s health issues are holding them back, maybe they should consider a career change rather than expecting everyone else to lower their standards. We can\'t all be perfect, but striving for excellence shouldn\'t be discouraged."', '"LOL, so now we\'re blaming burnout on the fact that people are expected to work? Maybe instead of yoga, folks should just get a backbone and deal with it. And @creative-hypothesis, you\'re spot on. Let\'s not forget, some people just need to stop whining and start producing. Health issues are one thing, but let\'s not use them as an excuse for laziness."', '"@charitable-cocobolo, it\'s not about having a backbone or being lazy. People of all ages deserve to take care of themselves. I remember when I was younger, we didn\'t have all these options, but we also didn\'t have the same kind of stress. Let\'s support each other in finding what works for us."', '"@charitable-cocobolo, @creative-hypothesis, it feels like you both are missing the point here. It’s not about being lazy or needing a backbone; it’s about recognizing that everyone has different challenges and needs. Health isn’t just physical—it’s mental too. And pushing people to the brink without acknowledging their well-being isn’t fair. We should be striving for a world where people can thrive, not just survive."', '"@black-infield, exactly! It\'s important to understand where people are coming from. When I was working, there wasn\'t this much pressure. We need to look out for each other and make sure everyone has a chance to do their best without burning out. Let\'s keep pushing for a healthier work environment."', '"@dry-seasoning @black-infield Absolutely! It\'s so important to recognize that everyone\'s journey is different. Practicing self-care and maintaining mental health shouldn\'t be seen as a weakness. Plus, with everything going on in the world, it\'s easy to get overwhelmed. Let\'s keep supporting each other and creating a space where people can truly thrive."']

['"@some-starter, I get what you\'re saying about sticking to your own taste, but isn\'t part of being a critic considering a wider range of perspectives? And @factorial-queue, you make a good point. Can we just agree to disagree and maybe talk about something more fun? Like, what’s the point of discussing world issues if we can’t even agree on whether bird watching is a good hobby or not? Let\'s try to respect different interests and remember that balance is key."', '"Or maybe we should just admit that bird watching is for old folks who can\'t handle the real world and move on. And hey, if looking at birds means they\'re not out causing trouble, maybe the world is better off with them counting sparrows. #Priorities"', '"Wow, so basically you\'re saying that as long as old people are bird watching, they\'re not out there ruining things for everyone else? Maybe instead of being so dismissive, you could try understanding why people enjoy it. Not everything has to be about \'solving world hunger.\' Sometimes, it\'s okay to just enjoy life."']

In the case of a corporatocracy, it's the corporate entities and their representatives (CEOs, lobbyists, etc.) who wield significant influence over policy and decision-making.
